speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, and today is the 28th of August. The-- Joshua Degani is the-- is being interviewed. Mr. Degani, what was your expectation of your arrival in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always in my mind, in spite of the fact that I knew that my father's not here anymore, that family will be together. I became-- I became aware that my father died in Yugoslavia. I was not aware yet about my mother and my children, and there was some simmering hope that we can see each other until I learned that this is not more possible. How exactly, I don't remember, but at one point I learned about my mother and sisters over there and that there is no hope anymore. And later on, I learned, as I have mentioned before, from documentation that they are not anymore alive. This was one of my hopes, but this hope went, and then we had-- I had to go my own way. Also, my brother had to go his-- his way. We had to go together to make a new life, and we were helped in Israel, in this respect. When we arrived there, we were received by the representative of those who acted with the Jewish youth, and we went to [INAUDIBLE], which is-- which we selected. We could select between a religious or a non-religious place, but we selected-- elected a religious place. And one consideration was that we were told there are wonderful teachers there. And, of course, religion was also a point because at that time, we were traditional and religious. And this brought us to [INAUDIBLE], and over there, we had the best study years-- or I had the best study years of my life. We had excellent, outstanding teachers. It was in all respects a place where they really tried to make our life spirituality easy. Also, we worked hard. We studied hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was agriculture, work which we did, and we progressed. And we also learned about the-- the war, the advances in the war, and that we are more and more successful-- I mean, our side, the British and the-- the Americans are successful and-- and progressing and-- against the aggression of the Nazi regime. And this was one thing we hoped will-- help will happen soon. And indeed, while we were there, the war came to an end in 1945, and this was about the time when our studies in [INAUDIBLE] ended. My brother stayed a little while longer, and I went on to work for a few years in agriculture and buildings in Israel, in Sde Ya'akov, and later went to Tel Aviv. And at one point, I had to quit this agriculture and went into another field where I felt that I can do much more, which was in the [? managerial ?] field. I studied more. I took courses more. I knew by then four languages, Hebrew-- and we studied the Bible in Hebrew. I knew German, and I knew English, and I knew Yiddish from home. My grandmother spoke Yiddish. And so I could advance-- begin a new field. But soon, you see, Israeli-- Israel was founded, and I joined the army. And I joined the army, and not to prolong the story, but I went through the ranks. I advanced to the rank of captain, and I was happy that I could participate in the success of [INAUDIBLE]. I participated in two wars. It was the war of [HEBREW], which was a-- first war in 1948. And then there was a Suez War in 1956, I believe. And later on, when I joined after the war a steamship company as a manager of their administration department, I was delegated to the New York branch. And since they diminished their activities in Israel on the steamship vessels, especially passenger vessels-- so there was no anymore a great job for me, so we decided to stay here in the United States. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You met your wife in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in Israel, and we are happily married for the last 47 and 1/2 years. In two and a half years, God willing, we will celebrate our 50th anniversary. And we-- just a few weeks ago, we got an invitation from the [INAUDIBLE] Leipzig to come there and to commemorate the 50th or 51st-- oh, one of the following 50th anniversary of the Kristallnacht. And we are scheduled to live there for one week, visit by the Leipzig government on-- in beginning November.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is not your first trip back.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is not my first trip. I was there before, and the reason of my first trip was that finally, when Germany was joined together, the east and the west, I did not have to go through procedures, and I could travel directly to Leipzig through Berlin. And my purpose was there to visit the cemetery, which has some of my ancestors also. I went there in spite that I was alone. I said Kaddish there. I took pictures there. And I met with the representative of the Israeli [INAUDIBLE]. We remained friends all the times. I took pictures, which I will show you later. And again, they know that I am coming because they signed the letter together with the head of the [INAUDIBLE] in the government of the Leipzig [INAUDIBLE] Oberburgermeister. So I am glad to follow this invitation, together with my wife, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: This group is still in-- in existence? What is their purpose now, the Israel--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Israeli-- it is a purpose to force-- as there are not many Jews in Leipzig at this time. There were not many when I visited, and according to the last report, there were approximately 60 or 70 families or 70 families who remained there. But the one synagogue which was then left alone because it was amidst other buildings-- left alone-- was not burned down at that time was in the [GERMAN], and this is still in existence, was refurbished just recently. And this is where the prayers are conducted on the high holidays. And this is apparently where the commemoration also will partially take place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your feeling when you returned to Leipzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sad. It was a sad feel, but I knew when I go there it will be sad because this is a place where I lived, and I wanted to see where we lived, where my father, mother, sisters, relatives lived. I wanted to see as many graves as I could of family members and others and get an impression what is there now. I found many cases which were rebuilt, many places which are still barren. Nothing's there. And I could not find our house. It was not our house-- it was not our previous address house. And I took pictures of the places which were nearby. They still have the same name, [INAUDIBLE]. And they still have the same name, Nordstrasse, where we lived. But our house, or where we lived, are not there anymore. It is either barren or there is something else. I believe in one spot is a hotel. Where we lived in the Nordstrasse is a modern hotel built. And [GERMAN], I could not identify where our place was, but I could estimate where it was, and I took pictures there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any fear about being in Leipzig or in Germany at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I already understood that the-- there is this denial feelings that everybody who I met there would say, I did not do it. I did not participate. I did not even know, which is true partially, partially, but not wholly. Many knew. Many participated. And many are responsible for it. Who is not so responsible, apparently, is the children. You see, the children-- and many are now the children of those. And you see the government of Israel does work with Germany, and Germany wants to work with Jewish people. We got an invitation, and see, punishment was given to most, not all, of those because they lost the war. They had [? their ?] victims. Not that I'm feeling elated that there are victims or that-- or dead people or maimed people, but it's a fact of life. They started the war, and they tried to overcome big powers, and moral-- moral-- moral elements in the wars and legal elements in the war, and they could not succeed. And I don't think that ever somebody will succeed to do this. And many books were written. You see, after the Nuremberg Trial, there is-- in Nuremberg in 1947, they wrote 42-- 42 volume about the Nuremberg, about the trial of the accused. And these books have many parts of the Holocaust in it. And later on, in Washington, I believe, there-- another 15 volumes written about the trials at Nuremberg. And again, all the elements which many tried-- or some tried to say it did not happen, many elements were truly described by the Nazis themselves. And this is-- I-- I read what I read, and I know it was written. And when time permits, I will go more and more over this-- the-- the literature. And I, myself, acquired quite a number of literary things, like which describes-- by Shirer, which describes exactly what happened, and it is in one volume, short but clear-- clearly stated. You see, so the restatement would be just a repetition, but it is all written. Or when I came-- when I visited England, I wrote-- bought a booklet which describes the Holocaust, and it is very clear and describes so many things accurately as I knew them and accurately as [INAUDIBLE] taken from authentic records. You see, so it is all in records. Or when later on, other research books what were done by famous people-- Abba Eban in Israel created tapes about Israel and what happened and what the background is. And there are in his description what-- what I-- matters which stem from the Holocaust experience. And last and not least, the most praise for the act done by Steven Spielberg, that he came out with this concept to create Shoah, or Holocaust. This is a wonderful, wonderful act, wonderful humanitarian act which will live forever, and I'm really grateful to him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I collected many of these matters, which I say my children and grandchildren-- they could see, could view this. And I will also attempt in future years, if I live, to enhance this core action which you do now, which you record for me. I want to do many more things, quite a number of more. I don't say you have to watch it constantly. I say-- and here remember one Jewish writer, [INAUDIBLE]. He said to his children, once a year, if you cannot say Kaddish, at least watch or read my books, read a story. So I say also here to my children, grandchildren, and whoever, once in a while, take it and see it and read-- read about it so that you know that something happened in history, a dreadful thing which will never happen again. And I pray to God that it should not happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm very happy to introduce my beloved wife, Leah, my daughter, Ora, my first grandson, Dylan, and my second grandson, David. They are a wonderful family. I love them all very, very dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm honored that my boys have gotten to meet their grandparents. I didn't get a chance to meet my grandparents, as you know my father's history now. And I'm glad that they have the sense of continuity and love in our family. You guys want to say hi? And-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to give your grandpa a hug?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to give me a hug, Dylan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give [? Saba ?] a hug, guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am so happy for this wonderful opportunity that all of us-- almost all of us-- are together today, and we love our-- our-- my husband and our-- my daughter's father and my grandchildren's [? saba. ?] They love him so much, and we all love him so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Very good. You want to say something, Dylan? And do you want to say something, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: What were the-- [INAUDIBLE] say-- oh, yeah. What were the weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the war, he wants to know. Tell [? Saba ?] you love him.

speaker: interviewer
text: The weapons in the war? That--

speaker: interviewee
text: That Grandpa was involved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask Grandpa. Grandpa knows the answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandpa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a lot of weapons.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can tell him [INAUDIBLE] and--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a war, there are weapons, but we do not want any more wars, right? We want to speak about peace, always peace, OK? And I hope you will encounter peace in your lives-- a lot of peace, peacetime, and be happy all the time, grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we hope that there will be soon peace in Israel between Israel and the Arab neighbors. Let's hope for a world of peace, like [? Saba ?] said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell [? Saba ?] and [INAUDIBLE] that you love them, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to look at these cards right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now he wants to look at his cards. I don't blame him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I dropped.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, [? Diane. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Steve. Thank you, [? Anita. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I would like to show you a few documents and pictures, not all, but some, to get a complete picture. This first one is of my-- my grandfather's grave in Leipzig, the one I was named after. He was called [INAUDIBLE] Brenner, and this is also the name of my grandmother, Brenner. And it is in Leipzig, and he died one year before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my recent visit to Leipzig, I could find an old telephone book and an old what is called here yellow pages with my father's name and activity. One is the telephone and address, together with his name, Habergrutz, in Humboldtstrasse 27. The telephone book-- and the other one, which is the yellow pages, where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the details of activity are mentioned. Again, Habergrutz, Chaim, textile, Humboldstrasse, [GERMAN]. All mentioned in this ad. Right here, we have two highly important documents, which I have received from the Israeli [INAUDIBLE] Leipzig-- one in 1956, and the other one in 1957, which show and certify that my mother and my three sisters were deported to Riga in 1942. And the second document shows that my grandmother was deported to Belzec in Poland, also in 1942, a few months later. Here is the last picture of my father which remained in my possession. My father was murdered in Dachau in 1941. And the other pictures are of my three sisters. Here are the two sisters, the eldest, Miriam, and Rosa. And the following picture, Rosa and my mother, done before I left Leipzig. As I mentioned before, Miriam, Rosa, and my mother and-- were deported to Riga in 1942. Miriam, Rosa, and Mindel are my three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have these pictures with you on your trip to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I did. Yes, I did. Here's my little sister again. Her name is Mindel. And as I said, she was deported to Riga in 1942. Once more, the date of birth-- Miriam was born on 21st of December, 1928. Rosa was born on 30th of April in 1937, and Mindel, this one, was born on 22nd of December in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was three years old when she was in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was three years old when she was deported and murdered. And here are some postcards-- I have more in my hands-- written by my mother and my sister for my grandmother in 1941. The writing is as clear as it would have been written today. Very sharp still after 54 years in my possession. Here, you can recognize the [GERMAN], or stamp by the Nazis on some of them. And I have one which certain items were cut out. But these letters are-- or postcards are very, very memorable. I will read one of them. They are written all in German, but I will read one of them in translation. My-- from my mother, may she rest in peace. 'My beloved and dearest children, I have received today your letter. My heart is going out to you, and I'm always happy to hear from you and to get from you mail. I'm-- I'm happy that, thank God, it-- you are fine, and I'm also elated that you write so punctually all the time. And it is also beautiful that both of you write on one postcard. This is the right way to do it. And you-- I know that you are wonderful, golden, and brave children, but I wish from the depths of my heart that you-- it should be always good with you. I ask you, dear children, never worry about us.' [SPEAKING GERMAN], in German. This is very interesting, such statement. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We should not worry about us. 'You children should watch your health all the time. And I hope that with the help of God, we will see each other soon. We regret very much that you have so little money. We obviously don't have enough money. But maybe I can send you some, a little. I am also glad that you had a nice trip. Dear children, watch out yourself very well, and keep together.' 'At this point'-- and this was written in 1941-- 'nothing seems to be important here. All the best wishes from all my heart, stay healthy and well, and there is another letter-- a letter for you on the way. I always write to you with a return voucher that you should not pay money for-- for the postage. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Kisses and best regards and love and from me, [INAUDIBLE], and sister, kiss-- and kisses from Miriam,' my eldest sister.
